summary:
Mughal Miniature Painting (c. 1555–1800 AD) was one of the great artistic achievements of the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] — a distinctive Indo-Persian pictorial tradition that combined Persian manuscript painting techniques with Indian color traditions and subject matter, producing works of extraordinary refinement commissioned by Mughal emperors for royal libraries (kitabkhana) and presented as diplomatic gifts. The tradition was established when the emperor [[event:3233|Humayun]] brought Persian master painters Mir Sayyid Ali and Abdus Samad from the Safavid court to India; under [[event:2381|Akbar]], the imperial atelier (karkhana) employed over 100 painters who produced illuminated manuscripts of Hindu epics, Persian poems, and historical chronicles — most notably the Hamzanama, with 1,400 large paintings on cloth.

The Mughal style evolved distinctly under successive emperors. Akbar's workshop emphasized narrative drama, bold composition, and the representation of the natural world with unprecedented naturalism. Jahangir (r. 1605–1627) was the most personally engaged patron: a keen naturalist who required his court painters to capture exotic animals and plants with scientific accuracy, his court produced the most celebrated individual portraits in Mughal art and some of the most sensitive nature paintings in Asian history. [[event:2506|Shah Jahan's]] court refined the style toward greater formality and jewel-like color. The tradition declined under [[event:2507|Aurangzeb's]] austere Islam but survived in regional schools at Rajput courts and eventually influenced Company painting (Indian artists working for British patrons) in the 18th and 19th centuries, bridging Mughal and modern Indian artistic traditions.
